sitting_overview: Industrial and economic policy ran through the day: the Chancellor set out a package of measures in response to rising costs, with funds for the chemicals and ceramics industries, and the Commons gave a Second Reading to the Bill that would bring British Steel into public ownership. The Foreign Office also made statements on Iran, the Strait of Hormuz and the wider region.

highlights.summary: The Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, froze fuel duty, suspended tariffs on more than 100 foods and cut VAT on summer attractions, in a package funded by a change to the taxation of foreign branch profits.
highlights.key_quote: Today I am taking action by suspending tariffs on over 100 different foods sold in supermarkets. I am clear that I expect supermarkets to pass those savings on in full to their customers.
highlights.analysis: The Chancellor announced a freeze in fuel duty, a cut in red diesel duty, a higher tax-free mileage rate, the suspension of tariffs on more than 100 foods, a temporary VAT cut on summer attractions and children's meals, and funds of £350 million for chemicals and £120 million for ceramics. She said the package would be paid for by a change to the taxation of foreign branch profits, which the Government expects to raise hundreds of millions of pounds a year.

debate_map.quote: the inevitable U-turn has finally come
For six months, we Liberal Democrats have been calling for an emergency temporary VAT cut for hospitality, leisure and attractions, and our pledge was fully funded.
have the Government modelled the job losses that may occur as a result of this change in the oil and gas taxation regime?

highlights.summary: The Business Secretary, Peter Kyle, opened the Second Reading of the Bill that would bring British Steel into public ownership.
highlights.key_quote: Our determination now is that the future may best be served by full public ownership in the national interest, not because of ideology, but because of practical pragmatism.
highlights.analysis: The Bill gives Ministers power to bring steel companies, including British Steel at Scunthorpe, into public ownership where a public interest test is met. The Conservatives opposed it with a reasoned amendment. The Bill passed its Second Reading.

debate_map.quote: Would the interests of one town where a steel facility is located be sufficient to justify the nationalisation of an entire company?
we do not believe that politicians or Whitehall bureaucrats should run businesses
More than 30 years ago—the House will not believe it—I left school and got a job in the steel industry.

highlights.constituency: Birmingham Hodge Hill and Solihull North
highlights.summary: Liam Byrne (LAB), who chairs the Business and Trade Committee, backed the Bill but said nationalisation would not succeed without lower energy costs, an EU tariff deal and firmer procurement.
highlights.key_quote: although the Secretary of State is proposing some perhaps welcome statism, he must not forget the statecraft that is needed to make a success of this Bill
highlights.analysis: Liam Byrne supported the Bill and set out six points: where investment for electric arc furnaces would come from, energy costs before the competitiveness scheme starts in 2027, a tariff deal with the EU, procurement, scrap supply, and consolidation of the industry.

debate_map.quote: there is no point in this Bill until we fix the electricity market in this country
We have launched a scrap working group, which will be dealing with that issue.

highlights.summary: Stuart Anderson (CON) said the 50% steel tariff starting on 1 July would hit a major stainless steel firm in his constituency for grades Britain cannot make; the Business Secretary agreed to look into it.
highlights.key_quote: The UK cannot meet domestic market demand, and a huge gap will be created. Businesses such as Amodil will be forced to import, and tariffs will drive up costs by 50%.
highlights.analysis: Stuart Anderson said Amodil, a stainless steel stockholder in his constituency and one of the largest in the UK, holds 2,000 tonnes of steel that cannot be made in Britain, and that the new 50% tariff would price such firms out. Several Conservative MPs raised similar cases through the day. The Business Secretary said the tariffs were intended to protect domestic production, not goods the UK does not make, and undertook to look into the company personally.

debate_map.quote: With regard to Amodil, I will look into that company personally.
He will be bankrupt within six months if this measure goes ahead.
we will end up subsidising the car industry like we did back in the 1970s

highlights.summary: Alistair Carmichael (LD) asked why food manufacturing, the largest part of the manufacturing base, was excluded from the industrial strategy.
highlights.key_quote: The largest sector of the manufacturing base is food manufacturing, but it was deliberately left out of the industrial strategy.
highlights.analysis: Alistair Carmichael said food manufacturing faces rising input costs, especially energy, and cannot access the support the strategy offers other sectors, with food price inflation forecast to reach 9% or 10% this year. The Minister, Chris McDonald, said the strategy had selected the sectors with the greatest growth potential but that food manufacturing remained important. The point was raised again with the Chancellor later in the day.

debate_map.quote: At the time that the industrial strategy was established, the Government carefully selected those sectors that had the greatest growth potential for the economy
Energy costs are driving up food inflation, as are a lot of the other anti-business policies put forward by this Government, including not incorporating food manufacturing in the industrial strategy.

highlights.summary: Sarah Olney (LD) said Government procurement, meant under the industrial strategy to back innovation, instead favours large and US suppliers over British start-ups.
highlights.key_quote: the process of getting Government contracts is slow, risk-averse and structurally biased in its financial viability tests and paperwork requirements towards incumbents and US suppliers.
highlights.analysis: Sarah Olney said technology start-ups find the contracting process slow and risk-averse, favouring established and US suppliers, and that this works against the strategy's own goal of greater innovation. The Minister, Chris McDonald, said the Government was committed to spending more taxpayers' money with UK firms and to reducing the bureaucracy that excludes small businesses.

debate_map.quote: this Government are committed to ensuring that as much as possible of UK taxpayers' money is spent in the UK.
If British taxpayer money is to be spent, then British industry must benefit.

highlights.summary: Justin Madders (LAB), whose constituency has a Vauxhall plant, said demand for electric vehicles will not meet the zero emission vehicle mandate, and asked the Treasury to review it.
highlights.key_quote: there is not, and there will not be, the consumer demand to meet the escalating demands of the ZEV mandate.
highlights.analysis: Justin Madders said the automotive sector faces a real crisis because of the zero emission vehicle mandate, and asked the Treasury to bring forward a review of it. The Business and Trade Committee chair, Liam Byrne, had said carmakers are subsidising electric vehicle sales by £5 billion a year. The Chief Secretary, Lucy Rigby, said the Chancellor might address the issue later.

debate_map.quote: UK automakers are having to subsidise demand by £5 billion a year
I appreciate that this is a really important issue

highlights.summary: Chris Hinchliff (LAB) said climate change is disrupting the global supply chains Britain relies on for essential goods, and called for the industrial strategy to rebuild home production.
highlights.key_quote: We are sleepwalking towards a situation in which this country can no longer guarantee the basic needs of its people.
highlights.analysis: Chris Hinchliff said that as climate breakdown accelerates, the international supply chains the UK relies on for essential goods may cease to exist, and asked Ministers to use the industrial strategy to return the economy to producing more of life's essentials. The Minister, Chris McDonald, said increasing manufacturing's share of the economy would improve national resilience.

debate_map.quote: Increasing the share of our economy that is dedicated to manufacturing will serve the ecological aims that he has mentioned, improve our national resilience

highlights.summary: Laurence Turner (LAB) told ministers a £500,000 cap on fines for breaching union access rules is 0.02% of the operating profit of Amazon's warehousing arm but a fifth of a mid-sized union's surplus.
highlights.key_quote: Capping fines at £500,000 equates to a potential liability of 0.02% of operating profits for Amazon's warehousing operations, but up to 20% of the surplus of a medium-sized trade union.
highlights.analysis: Laurence Turner said a £500,000 cap on penalties would be a trivial sum for a large employer like Amazon but a heavy one for a medium-sized union, and asked how that achieves a workable right of access. The Minister, Kate Dearden, said the enforcement mechanism must be fair and proportionate and would weigh the gravity of a breach and the size of the party.

debate_map.quote: The Government are clear that the enforcement mechanism must be fair, proportionate and adaptable

highlights.summary: Wendy Chamberlain (LD) said the documents released on Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's 2001 appointment as a trade envoy show he was given the role with no vetting.
highlights.key_quote: The files that we have seen show that there was no vetting by the Government and that, even then, no questions appear to have been asked.
highlights.analysis: The Government published 11 documents in response to a Humble Address on how Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was appointed a special representative for trade and investment. Wendy Chamberlain said the files showed no vetting and no questions asked, and asked whether Ministers had been aware of his connection to Jeffrey Epstein, which she said was already established at the time. The Minister, Chris Bryant, said the Government was co-operating with a police investigation into potential misconduct in public office.

debate_map.quote: I reiterate that the Government are fully co-operating with Thames Valley police in their investigation into potential misconduct in public office.
Where, if anywhere, are the documented concerns or challenge from officials or Ministers at the time?
as I think we have known for some time, no vetting was done

highlights.summary: The Foreign Office Minister, Hamish Falconer, updated the House on efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, closed by Iran, and on Gaza and the wider region.
highlights.key_quote: We cannot risk tens of millions of people going hungry because Iran has hijacked a vital international shipping lane.
highlights.analysis: The Foreign Office Minister said Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz was a breach of international law driving a global food crisis, and set out a multinational naval mission to support shipping. He condemned the treatment of detained flotilla activists by the Israeli Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir, and said humanitarian restrictions in Gaza were indefensible and must end.

debate_map.quote: No country should have the power to shut down a global artery of international commerce and trade.
when will we stop hesitating and take action to ensure that international law is respected?

highlights.debate: Imprisonment of Craig and Lindsay Foreman in Iran
highlights.summary: Tony Vaughan (LAB) led an adjournment debate on Craig and Lindsay Foreman, two British tourists jailed in Iran for 10 years for espionage and now on hunger strike.
highlights.key_quote: simply by speaking to people about what makes a good life, the Foremans were conducting espionage and attempting to overthrow the Iranian regime
highlights.analysis: Tony Vaughan said Craig and Lindsay Foreman, the mother and stepfather of one of his constituents, were convicted by a revolutionary court after forced confessions and solitary confinement, and are now on hunger strike in Evin prison. Members from both sides asked the Government to declare the detention arbitrary. The Minister, Hamish Falconer, said the couple were innocent tourists but that public pressure was not always the most effective route to a release.

debate_map.quote: Iran is a terrorist state with a record of holding people hostage for political leverage.
I cannot provide that assurance.
We will repeat their names in this House until they are released.

highlights.summary: Lee Barron (LAB) named a constituent killed at a Corby steelworks, with the man's family in the gallery, and called for stronger health and safety law.
highlights.key_quote: health and safety is not red tape, bureaucracy or a barrier to growth, but what keeps people safe at work
highlights.analysis: Lee Barron said Kenny Campbell Smart went to work at Corby steelworks on 13 January 2026 and never returned home, and named him so that his name would be recorded in Hansard. The man's widow and son watched from the gallery. The Leader of the House said good health and safety practices save lives and that the relevant Minister would set out further action.

debate_map.quote: Good health and safety practices save lives

highlights.summary: Imran Hussain (LAB) described a London march at which figures called for Muslims to leave the country, and asked for a debate on Islamophobia.
highlights.key_quote: The incitement of hatred and violence against British Muslims in the heart of our capital should worry us all.
highlights.analysis: Imran Hussain told the House that a weekend march had seen figures openly call for Muslims to leave the country, and asked for time to debate Islamophobia and the protection of British Muslim communities. The Leader of the House condemned the disorder and pointed to new legislation on protests outside places of worship.

debate_map.quote: I absolutely condemn any disorder and inappropriate language, whoever it comes from and whoever it is directed against.

sections.summary: The sitting opened at half-past nine with prayers.

Oral questions to the Secretary of State for Business and Trade, Peter Kyle, and his ministers. Members raised digital ID for businesses, ticket touts, Royal Mail services, the industrial strategy, support for pubs and hospitality, and the Employment Rights Act 2025. A concern raised across parties was that new steel tariffs and quotas due on 1 July would raise costs for manufacturers that import grades of steel not made in the UK.

An urgent question on the Government's plans on the cost of fuel for drivers. The Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Lucy Rigby, set out a freeze in fuel duty, a cut in red diesel duty and a vehicle excise duty holiday for heavy goods vehicles. The Speaker said the announcement should have been made to the House before the media.

The Leader of the House, Sir Alan Campbell, set out forthcoming business and answered questions from across the House. Members raised the independent review of access to banking, a constituent killed at a Corby steelworks, Islamophobia following a weekend march, and a wide range of constituency matters.

The Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, made an economic statement to the House. She announced a freeze in fuel duty, a cut in red diesel duty, a higher tax-free mileage rate, the suspension of tariffs on more than 100 foods, a temporary VAT cut on summer attractions and children's meals, and funds for the chemicals and ceramics industries, to be paid for by a change to the taxation of companies' foreign branch profits.

A Foreign Office statement on Iran, the Strait of Hormuz and the wider region. The Minister, Hamish Falconer, condemned the treatment of detained flotilla activists by an Israeli Minister, described the humanitarian restrictions in Gaza as indefensible, and set out diplomatic and naval efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Members from all parties called for stronger action.

The Minister for Trade, Chris Bryant, returned to a Humble Address by publishing documents on the 2001 appointment of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor as a special representative for trade and investment. The documents showed that no vetting was carried out. The Government said it was co-operating with a police investigation into potential misconduct in public office.

The Second Reading of the Bill giving the Government power to take steel companies, including British Steel at Scunthorpe, into public ownership. The Conservatives opposed it with a reasoned amendment. Members raised energy costs, the funding of new furnaces, and tariffs affecting manufacturers that use imported steel. The Bill passed its Second Reading.
A statutory instrument establishing the Hampshire and the Solent Combined County Authority was approved without debate.

An adjournment debate, led by Tony Vaughan, on Craig and Lindsay Foreman, two British tourists jailed in Iran for 10 years on espionage charges. Members from across the House asked the Government to declare the detention arbitrary. The Minister, Hamish Falconer, said the couple were innocent tourists and that the Government was working through all channels.
